texto: The mountain has left me feeling renewed, more content and positive than I’ve been for weeks, as if something has been given back after a long absence, as if my eyes have opened once again. For this time at least, I’ve let myself be rooted in the unshakable sanity of the senses, spared my mind the burden of too much thinking, turned myself outward to experience the world and inward to savor the pleasures it has given me.
explicacao: Metáfora atribuída a Richard Nelson: compara elementos do cotidiano para falar de passagem do tempo e prioridades. O trecho “The mountain has left me feeling renewed, more content and positive than I’ve been for weeks, as …” usa imagem concreta para lembrar que reflexao, meditation, mountains pedem movimento e interpretação pessoal, em tom de mountains e nature.

frase_original: The mountain has left me feeling renewed, more content and positive than I’ve been for weeks, as if something has been given back after a long absence, as if my eyes have opened once again. For this time at least, I’ve let myself be rooted in the unshakable sanity of the senses, spared my mind the burden of too much thinking, turned myself outward to experience the world and inward to savor the pleasures it has given me.

texto: What obligation is more binding than to protect the cherished, to defend whoever or whatever cannot defend itself, and to nurture in turn that which has given nourishment? I’m reminded of words written by John Seed, an Australian environmentalist. When he began considering these questions, he believed, “I am protecting the rain forest.” But as his thought evolved, he realized, “I am part of the rain forest protecting myself.
explicacao: Pergunta retórica de Richard Nelson que provoca limites e escolhas conscientes. Ao questionar “What obligation is more binding than to protect the cherished, to defend whoever or whatever cann…”, o texto convida o leitor a revisar crenças sobre saude-mental, environmental-conservation, nature, com foco em dia-chuvoso e termino.
